title: Volunteer for maintainer reviewer role
author: arturict
body: Hi maintainers—I'd like to help more consistently with maintainer-level review. I can help with PR reviews, formatting checks, and verifying translation PRs for consistency. If helpful, I can monitor a specific area (e.g., system design solutions or translation updates) and provide regular review turnaround.

title: Translation: Roman Urdu/Hindi
author: Ahtisham-1214
body:
I am from Pakistan, Native Urdu.

I would like to contribute in a translation in **Roman Urdu/Hindi**

In Roman Urdu we use English alphabets but create words/sentences of Urdu and we also include English words as well, so it's kind a mixture. **Roman Urdu** is understandable by Pakistanis, Indians, and I think Nepalis as well. So, it's a population of around 2b people. Through this contribution we can enable millions of people to learn easily.

Well, I prefer to read in English as I can understand but there's a lot people who find it difficult, for them giving their native translation does not make sense because they find it even harder because the word we are translating are not our language word. For example what would I say **Decision Tree**, **Machine Learning** and other technical words in Urdu/Hindi? Since, they are not Urdu/Hindi dictionary words. 

Since, we (Pakistanis, Indians) use Roman Urdu/Hindi in our daily life communication so it's better to have this translation, and to be honest those who find English docs, books difficult  Roman Urdu/Hindi is the best solution for them. Even with LLM's we communicate in Roman Urdu/Hindi

Well, I can do translation in Urdu as well, but the problem with Urdu translation is that even we Pakistanis don't prefer to read or write in Urdu because it is quite difficult and not everyone is good at Pure Urdu. So, we usually use Roman Urdu/Hindi every where, like in chats, communication, marketing and so on.
For example if I have to say my friend `Let's Play`.
We don't say in chats  `چلو کھیلتے ہیں` rather we say `Chalo khelte hain`

Finally, you might be wondering why I am kept mentioning Urdu/Hindi? Basically Pakistanis, Indians and Nepalis can communicate with each other in their own language like Indian in Hindi and Pakistani in Urdu. Well, these language are only different in writing but when it comes to speaking all of them are 90% same. That's why in countries like Pakistan, India, Nepal we communicate in **Roman Urdu/Hindi** even Afghanis can communicate in with us, the only difference in writing. So, this contribution will help a lot of people.

So, I would request you to allow me to contribute in this project to do **Roman Urdu/Hindi** translation.

_Thank you for your time and consideration!_

title: Project Showcase: Battle-Tested Patterns — 46 programming patterns extracted from production codebases, with precise source links and multi-language exercises
author: Totoro-jam
body:
**English**:

Hi everyone! Ive been following this repo for a while — its been a great reference for understanding system design trade-offs.

I built an open-source project called **[Battle-Tested Patterns](https://github.com/Totoro-jam/battle-tested-patterns)** that I think complements this primer nicely.

**The connection**: System Design Primer covers *high-level architecture* — scalability, CAP theorem, caching strategies, database replication, load balancing, and the trade-offs between them. These are the design principles behind large-scale systems. My project approaches the same systems from the *implementation layer* — the concrete programming patterns that bring those designs to life.

For example, System Design Primer discusses database replication and consistency patterns. Underneath those high-level designs, real databases use specific programming patterns to implement them: WAL + Checkpointing + B+ Tree for PostgreSQLs storage and recovery, MVCC for transaction isolation, LRU Cache for buffer management. My project extracts and teaches these patterns individually, each with precise source links to the production codebases that use them.

**What the project includes**:

- **46 patterns** across 5 categories: data structures, concurrency, system, memory, behavior
- **Precise GitHub source links**: Every pattern links to exact lines in production code (e.g., `ReactFiberFlags.js#L18-L22`)
- **46 interactive SVGs**: Clickable, animatable visualizations — step through an LRU eviction, watch a B+ Tree split
- **4-language implementations**: TypeScript, Rust, Go, Python — all runnable (1,073 test cases)
- **Production proof**: Every pattern has ≥2 verified references from projects like React, Linux kernel, Redis, PostgreSQL, Go runtime, Kafka
- **Bilingual**: Full English + Chinese documentation

| Category | Example Patterns |
|---|---|
| Data Structures | Bitmask, LRU Cache, B+ Tree, Bloom Filter, Skip List, Merkle Tree (11) |
| Concurrency | Semaphore, Actor Model, Work Stealing, MVCC, Event Loop (9) |
| System | Circuit Breaker, Rate Limiter, WAL, LSM Tree, Consistent Hashing (12) |
| Memory | Object Pool, Arena Allocator, Copy-on-Write, Reference Counting (8) |
| Behavioral | State Machine, Iterator, Diff/Patch, Visitor (6) |

**Screenshots**:

![Interactive LRU Cache visualization with time-travel controls](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/71a4bbe7-f5ab-474d-85ed-ed286f114254)

![Pattern detail — source links, code tabs, related patterns, challenge questions](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/93b6a5c3-d3f3-4de0-aa44-1915c6067c3b)

**Links**:
- GitHub: https://github.com/Totoro-jam/battle-tested-patterns
- Docs: https://totoro-jam.github.io/battle-tested-patterns/
- 中文文档: https://totoro-jam.github.io/battle-tested-patterns/zh/

(I recognize this is self-promotion, but the content is free, MIT-licensed, and genuinely complementary to what this primer teaches. If the maintainer feels it doesnt belong here, just let me know and I will close it.)

---

---

**中文**：

大家好，我一直在关注这个 repo，它在理解系统设计权衡方面是一个非常好的参考。

我构建了一个开源项目 **[Battle-Tested Patterns](https://github.com/Totoro-jam/battle-tested-patterns)**，我认为它与本 primer 是互补关系。

**两者的联系**：System Design Primer 覆盖的是*高层架构*——可伸缩性、CAP 定理、缓存策略、数据库复制、负载均衡以及它们之间的权衡取舍。这些是大型系统背后的设计原则。而我的项目则从*实现层*来审视同样的系统——那些将设计变为现实的具体编程模式。

举个例子，System Design Primer 讨论了数据库复制和一致性模式。在这些高层设计之下，真实的数据库使用特定的编程模式来实现它们：PostgreSQL 用 WAL + Checkpointing + B+ Tree 实现存储和恢复，用 MVCC 实现事务隔离，用 LRU Cache 管理缓冲池。我的项目将这些模式单独提取出来进行教学，每个模式都附有精确到源码位置的 GitHub 链接。

**项目包含**：

- **46 个模式**，分布在 5 个类别：数据结构、并发、系统、内存、行为
- **精确的 GitHub 源码链接**：每个模式都链到生产代码的精确行号（如 `ReactFiberFlags.js#L18-L22`）
- **46 个交互式 SVG 组件**：可点击可拖拽的动画——亲手操作 LRU 淘汰过程、观察 B+ Tree 分裂动画
- **4 种语言实现**：TypeScript、Rust、Go、Python——全部可运行（共 1,073 个测试用例）
- **生产验证**：每个模式至少有 2 个来自 React、Linux 内核、Redis、PostgreSQL、Go runtime、Kafka 等项目的经过验证的引用
- **中英双语**：完整的中文和英文学文档

| 分类 | 模式示例 |
|---|---|
| 数据结构 | Bitmask、LRU Cache、B+ Tree、Bloom Filter、Skip List、Merkle Tree（11个） |
| 并发 | Semaphore、Actor Model、Work Stealing、MVCC、Event Loop（9个） |
| 系统 | Circuit Breaker、Rate Limiter、WAL、LSM Tree、Consistent Hashing（12个） |
| 内存 | Object Pool、Arena Allocator、Copy-on-Write、Reference Counting（8个） |
| 行为 | State Machine、Iterator、Diff/Patch、Visitor（6个） |

**截图**：

![LRU Cache 交互动画——属性表、时间轴回放控制器、淘汰动画](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/71a4bbe7-f5ab-474d-85ed-ed286f114254)

![模式详情页——精确源码链接、代码标签页、相关模式、挑战问题](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/93b6a5c3-d3f3-4de0-aa44-1915c6067c3b)

**链接**：
- GitHub：https://github.com/Totoro-jam/battle-tested-patterns
- 英文文档：https://totoro-jam.github.io/battle-tested-patterns/
- 中文文档：https://totoro-jam.github.io/battle-tested-patterns/zh/

（我清楚这是自荐，但内容是完全免费、MIT 许可的，并且与本 primer 的内容确实互补。如果维护者觉得不合适，告知后我会主动关闭。）

author: tarekyahya2004-hue0

title: docs: multiple core sections outdated for 2025-2026 cloud-native practice
author: rushabhT3
body:
Hi maintainers — first, thank you for the primer. It's still the most-linked resource for system design interviews.

I consistently hitting gaps where the content reflects ~2015-2017 practices. These aren't nitpicks — they lead to wrong design choices in interviews today.

**Outdated / missing areas found by section:**

**1. Latency numbers (Appendix)**
- Still uses 2012 figures: L1 0.5ns, SSD random 150µs, 1Gbps network
- 2025 reality: L1 ~1ns, NVMe random ~20µs, 100Gbps datacenter RTT ~0.1-0.5ms, 1MB SSD sequential ~0.1ms (not 1ms). Candidates using old numbers are off by 10-100x on back-of-envelope.

**2. Load Balancer + CDN**
- Covers hardware LB, HAProxy, round-robin, Layer 4/7 only
- Missing: service mesh (Envoy/Istio) for east-west, global anycast, edge compute (Workers/Lambda@Edge), HTTP/3 QUIC, and cloud-managed LBs (ALB/NLB/Global Accelerator). No mention of connection-ID load balancing for QUIC.

**3. Database / NoSQL**
- Focuses on master-slave replication, MySQL query cache (removed in MySQL 8.0), and "graph DBs are new/not widely used"
- Missing entirely: Distributed SQL (Spanner, CockroachDB, Yugabyte), vector databases (pgvector, Pinecone), lakehouse (Iceberg/Delta), time-series DBs, and NewSQL consensus patterns. DynamoDB still labeled "document-oriented" only.

**4. Asynchronism & Communication**
- Message queues limited to Redis/RabbitMQ/SQS with no delivery semantics
- Kafka only in appendix, no partitions/retention; Pulsar missing
- No exactly-once, outbox pattern, or idempotency discussion
- RPC mentions Thrift/Protobuf but not gRPC; no GraphQL, WebSockets, or HTTP/3

**5. Security & Availability**
- Security section explicitly says "could use some updates" — still only "encrypt at rest, sanitize inputs"
- Missing: zero-trust, mTLS, OAuth2/OIDC, secrets management (Vault/KMS)
- Availability only covers active-passive and 2-node active-active; no multi-region active-active, chaos engineering, or SLO-based design
- Observability: only Dapper mentioned, no OpenTelemetry, traces/metrics/logs

**6. Application layer / Real-world**
- Examples are pre-2015 (Twitter 2013 architecture)
- No containers, Kubernetes, serverless, or service mesh

**Impact:** Junior engineers following the primer literally will design around RAID-style disks, master-slave MySQL, and hardware LBs — patterns that are red flags in 2025 FAANG/staff loops, where interviewers expect cloud-native defaults.

title: [Translation] Urdu (اردو) - README-ur.md
author: maroofiums
body:
## Urdu Translation

Hi, I'd like to maintain the Urdu (اردو) translation of the System Design Primer.

### About Me
- Name: Maroof
- Based in Karachi, Pakistan
- CS student and ML/AI Engineer
- Native Urdu speaker 

### Translation Plan
- File: `README-ur.md`
- Approach: Technical terms (DNS, CDN, Load Balancer, etc.) will remain in English for clarity; explanatory prose and headings will be translated into Urdu
- Code blocks will be kept as-is


I will keep this thread updated with progress. Happy to take this on as an active maintainer.

Related: #28 (Add Translation)

title: Project Showcase: AI financial chart analysis tool
author: xiaohoumin3-alt
body:
Hi donnemartin community,

I built an open-source AI tool that analyzes Gold (XAUUSD) and Bitcoin (BTCUSD) across 5 timeframes using YOLOv8.

Features:
- YOLO object detection on candlestick charts
- Multi-timeframe analysis (M5/M15/M30/H1/H4)
- Auto-publishing to Telegram channels

GitHub: https://github.com/xiaohoumin3-alt/ChartScanAI
Free trial: https://t.me/ChartScanAI

author: chengjunhua465-create
body:
Hey! I built a dev workflow automation toolkit for AI-assisted development. It comes with:

• Pre-built prompts for Claude Code, Codex, and DeepSeek

If you're building with AI tools, it might save you time. Free trial available.

title: Aide d’urgence code à corriger
author: balabolka01
body:
import numpy as np
import time

class SpartanTacticalConsole:
    def __init__(self):
        # Liste des mots-clés les plus puissants (calculés par IA)
        self.power_keywords = ["Automated B2B", "Growth Hack 2026", "Quantum Leads"]
        self.nodes = ["Virginia-US", "Nairobi-KE", "Manila-PH"]
        self.stealth_active = True

    def deploy_stealth_ads mode furtif"""
        print("="*60)
        print(" [!] DÉPLOIEMENT DE LA CAMPAGNE ALOHA_MARK")
        print("="*60)
        
        for keyword in self.power_keywords:
            # Choix du nœud de sortie pour éviter le "Ad-Blocking" géographique
            node = np.random.choice(self.nodes)
            
            print(f"[*] Mot-clé : {keyword}")
            print(f"[*] Injection via Node : {node}")
            print(f"[*] Masquage : Googlebot-Proxy v2.1")
            
            # (Bidding)
            bid_price = np.random.uniform(0.10, 0.50)
            print(f"[+] Enchère optimisée : {bid_price:.2f}$ / clic")
            print("-" * 30)
            time.sleep(0.5)

    def activate_kill_switch(self):
        """Nettoyage complet des traces de session"""
        print("\n[!] SÉCURITÉ : ACTIVATION DU KILL SWITCH")
        # Ici, on purgerait les logs et la mémoire vive
        print("[+] Toutes les empreintes (Fingerprints) ont été effacées.")

# --- LANCEMENT ---
console = SpartanTacticalConsole()
console.deploy_stealth_ads()
console.activate_kill_switch()

title: Suggestion: Add a structured system design learning path companion
author: usubhash
body:
Hi maintainers — thank you for this incredible primer.

Would you consider adding [StreamPrep](https://streamprep.dev) as a companion resource for candidates who need a structured progression plan in addition to reference material?

Why this might help:
- Primer = deep reference and concepts
- StreamPrep = guided sequence and pacing
- Useful for learners preparing on a timeline

Happy to open a minimal PR if this is aligned.

Suggested text:
`- [StreamPrep](https://streamprep.dev) - Structured interview roadmap with guided progression, including system design practice.`

title: Suggestion: section on cost-aware system design (per-seat vs per-call tradeoffs)
author: patl4588
body:
Suggestion / discussion: would a section on **cost-aware system design** be welcome? Specifically:
- Fan-out patterns and how per-call billing ends up cheaper than per-seat at scale
- Cap-and-shed patterns when using metered third-party APIs
- Tradeoffs between flat-rate vendor contracts vs pay-per-call

I've been collecting real examples at `patl4588/awesome-saas-replacements`. Happy to draft a PR with a new subsection if this is in scope for the primer — or leave it out if you'd rather keep the focus narrower.

Thanks for this repo — it's helped a lot of people.
